text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 28 July 2019

Dr Susan
University College Hospital
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Susan,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/Diagnoses: Longstanding epilepsy with prior head injury history not fully documented; cognitive slowing reported by family; vitamin D insufficiency; ex-smoker now reduced to occasional use; recent migrant to the UK learning NHS pathways with occasional interpreter support.

Medication: Currently taking Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily; Lamotrigine 50 mg morning and 100 mg evening; Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily; Omeprazole 20 mg daily; Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. Previously tried Sodium Valproate (discontinued due to weight gain and tremor) and Topiramate (stopped for word-finding difficulty and low mood).

I reviewed him in clinic today accompanied by his mother and an NHS link worker who provides ad hoc interpreter support. He has recently registered with a GP and is getting familiar with the referral and prescription processes. He reports improved sleep routine and is attending community physiotherapy for balance and deconditioning after a prolonged period of inactivity prior to arrival in the UK. On examination, gait was slow but stable without aids, no focal weakness, and mild action tremor of the hands. Cognition: oriented to person and place; variable short-term recall per family, consistent with their reports.

Seizures: The family keep a simple calendar log on their phone. In Mar he had a run of three seizures within half an hour (not full generalised tonic-clonic, fluctuating in intensity, resolved without medication). In Apr there was 7 further seizures at night, and in Jul another during physiotherapy (unwitnessed by mother). No injuries reported, and there has been no use of rescue medication. Triggers suspected by the family include poor sleep and missed meals; no intercurrent infections noted.

Adherence and tolerability: He reports full adherence to Levetiracetam and Lamotrigine with a weekly pill organiser set up by his mother. Mood has been stable with occasional irritability, which he attributes to stress of navigating new systems. No rashes or diplopia. Weight stable. No falls.

Investigations: Prior imaging from overseas is not available. We have requested prior records via the GP. Routine blood tests (U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D) to be arranged in primary care as he has not had baseline bloods since arrival. ECG previously normal in the community clinic.

Plan:
- Continue current anti-seizure medications without change.
- Provide written seizure first-aid guidance to the family and physiotherapy team; discuss when to seek emergency care.
- GP to arrange baseline bloods and vitamin D monitoring; share results via clinic email.
- Community epilepsy nurse referral for education, including medication routines and NHS access, with interpreter support as needed.
- Request overseas records through the GP and consider future imaging once prior data reviewed.
- Follow-up in 6 months or sooner if seizure clusters recur or injuries occur.

Yours sincerely,

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides explicit counts by months in the current year: "In Mar he had a run of three seizures within half an hour... In Apr there was 7 further seizures at night, and in Jul another..." This totals 3 + 7 + 1 = 11 seizures over the period March through July inclusive, which is 5 months. No other seizure counts or frequencies are given. Using the rule to prioritize explicit numeric expressions and to compute a frequency over the stated timeframe, we derive 11 per 5 months.
seizure_frequency_number: In Mar he had a run of three seizures within half an hour... In Apr there was 7 further seizures at night, and in Jul another
